1.synthesis: When the Sabbath is past, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome buy spices to go and anoint him (v.1). Very early on the first day of the week they go to the tomb at sunrise, asking one another who will roll away the stone (vv.2–3). Looking up, they see the stone has already been rolled back — for it was very large (v.4). Entering the tomb, they see a young man sitting on the right side, dressed in a white robe, and they are alarmed (v.5). He says: "Do not be alarmed. You seek Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen; he is not here. See the place where they laid him. But go, tell his disciples and Peter that he is going before you to Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you" (vv.6–7). They flee and say nothing to anyone, for they were afraid (v.8).
1.historical_context: The visit of the women with spices (aromatic compounds to mask the odor of decomposition and honor the body) reflects Jewish burial customs; the stone would have been a wheel-shaped boulder rolled in a channel across the tomb entrance — for a new-hewn tomb, estimated weight up to a ton. The "first day of the week" is Sunday, the third day after Friday's crucifixion (Jewish inclusive counting). The young man in a white robe (v.5) is generally understood as an angel — white garments are angelic attire in the OT and Jewish apocalyptic (Dan 10:5-6; 2 Macc 11:8). The mention of "Peter" specifically (v.7) singles out the one who denied Jesus three times — his restoration begins here. The Galilean promise recalls Jesus's words of 14:28: "after I am raised up, I will go before you to Galilee" — the angel cites this as "just as he told you."
1.literary_structure: Mark's Gospel ends at v.8 in the earliest and most reliable manuscripts — the abrupt ending (the women flee and say nothing, for they were afraid: ephobounto gar) is either the most striking narrative ending in ancient literature or evidence that the original ending was lost. The longer endings (vv.9–20) are secondary, assembled from the other Gospels and Acts. The ending at v.8 is consistent with Mark's emphasis on fear and awe before the divine (4:41; 5:15; 9:6) and with the messianic-secret motif: the secret is now fully disclosed, and the silence of the witnesses at the moment of disclosure is the last irony of a Gospel that has been full of unexpected silence and unexpected speech. The reader knows what the women cannot yet speak.
1.theological_application: "He has risen; he is not here" (v.6). The empty tomb is not the resurrection — it is the evidence that points to what cannot be directly observed. The announcement is the interpretive key: the absence of the body is explained not by theft (the counter-narrative of Matthew 28:13) but by resurrection. The angel's instruction to go and tell (v.7) against the women's silence (v.8) creates the narrative gap that the reader must fill: the Gospel we hold in our hands is the testimony that eventually did get told. The fear that silences the women in v.8 is the same fear of the Lord that is the beginning of wisdom — overwhelming encounter with the divine presence. It is not the last word but the penultimate one.
1.christology: "Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen" (v.6). The risen one is identified with the crucified one — the resurrection does not replace the crucifixion but vindicates it and the one who underwent it. He is going before them to Galilee (v.7): the shepherd who was struck (14:27) is going before his scattered flock, precisely as he promised. The Galilean ministry with which the Gospel opened — "Jesus came into Galilee, proclaiming the gospel of God" (1:14) — is now resumed from the other side of death: he goes before them as the Risen Lord into the same territory where it all began. The circle closes and opens at once.

9.synthesis: The longer ending (vv.9–20), though not in the earliest manuscripts, has been part of the canonical Mark since the second century and is included in this synthesis as received tradition. Early on the first day of the week, Jesus appears first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons (v.9). She goes and tells those who had been with him, now mourning and weeping; they hear that he is alive and has been seen by her, but they do not believe (vv.10–11). Jesus appears in another form to two disciples walking in the country; they go back and tell the rest, but they too are not believed (vv.12–13). The longer ending's opening section thus narrates two resurrection appearances characterized by the disciples' persistent unbelief — the opposite of the faith the resurrection calls for.
9.historical_context: The longer ending of Mark (vv.9–20) is a second-century compilation drawing on Luke 24, John 20, and Acts. The appearance to Mary Magdalene corresponds to John 20:14–18; the appearance to two disciples walking corresponds to Luke 24:13–35 (the Emmaus road). The identification of Mary Magdalene as a woman "from whom he had cast out seven demons" (v.9) appears earlier in Luke 8:2; the description in the longer ending treats her as if newly introduced, suggesting the secondary compiler used a different source for this section. The persistent unbelief of the disciples in vv.10–11 and v.13 reflects a tension the longer ending will resolve in the commissioning scene (vv.14–20).
9.literary_structure: The longer ending is constructed as a sequence of appearances with decreasing unbelief: Mary is not believed (v.11) → two disciples are not believed (v.13) → Jesus appears to the eleven and rebukes their unbelief directly (v.14) → they are commissioned and go out (vv.15–20). This is a narrative arc from absence and disbelief to presence and mission. The repetition of "not believed" in vv.11 and 13 echoes the disciples' failure to understand the passion predictions and the transfiguration — the resurrection does not automatically produce faith; it requires the personal encounter with the risen Jesus to generate conviction.
9.theological_application: Mary Magdalene's testimony — to the disciples who had been with Jesus — is dismissed as unbelievable (v.11). The Gospel is first proclaimed and first rejected in the same moment. That the first resurrection witnesses are women whose testimony was not legally valid in first-century Jewish courts, and that their report is dismissed by the disciples themselves, underscores a consistent pattern in Mark: truth comes from unexpected sources and is rejected by those who should welcome it. The disciples' unbelief at the resurrection mirrors the Pharisees' unbelief at the miracles — the evidence is present; the faith is not yet there.
9.christology: "He appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons" (v.9). The risen Jesus appears first to the woman most dramatically transformed by his ministry — the one from whom the greatest brokenness was removed. This is not coincidental: the resurrection is the completion of the healing and liberation ministry of Jesus's entire career. The same Lord who expelled seven demons now appears as the risen one; the same power that freed her from possession is the power that broke death's hold. Her testimony about his resurrection is the extension of her testimony about his healing — the same Lord acting in the same pattern: entering into the worst situations and bringing life.

14.synthesis: Afterward Jesus appears to the eleven as they recline at table; he rebukes their unbelief and hardness of heart for not believing those who had seen him after he rose (v.14). "Go into all the world and proclaim the gospel to the whole creation. Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned" (vv.15–16). Signs will accompany those who believe: casting out demons, speaking new tongues, handling serpents, drinking something deadly without harm, laying hands on the sick who will recover (vv.17–18). After speaking to them, the Lord Jesus is taken up into heaven and sits at the right hand of God (v.19). They go out and preach everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word through the accompanying signs (v.20).
14.historical_context: The commission of vv.15–16 is the Markan analogue to Matthew 28:18–20 (the Great Commission) and to Acts 1:8. The scope — "all the world... the whole creation" — is wider even than Matthew's "all nations," extending the mission to the entire cosmos. The signs listed in vv.17–18 correspond to what the disciples had already done in Jesus's name during the mission of 6:7–13 and to what Acts records in the early church (demon exorcism: Acts 16:18; tongues: Acts 2:4; snake-handling: Acts 28:3–6; healing: Acts 28:8). The ascension (v.19) is described with the language of Psalm 110:1 ("sits at the right hand of God") — the same text Jesus had cited at his trial (14:62): what he predicted then is narrated as completed now.
14.literary_structure: The longer ending concludes the Gospel by resolving the unbelief theme: Jesus appears directly, rebukes the unbelief, commissions the now-believing disciples, and the commission is carried out — "they went out and preached everywhere" (v.20). The ascension and session at the right hand of God (v.19) fulfills the trial prediction of 14:62 and the Psalm-110 interpretation of 12:36 — the Gospel's christological claims are vindicated in the final narrative event. "The Lord working with them" (v.20: synergountos) implies that the ascended Christ continues active ministry through the apostolic mission — the boundary between earth and heaven is permeable to his ongoing agency.
14.theological_application: "Go into all the world and proclaim the gospel to the whole creation" (v.15). The universal scope of the mission is grounded in the universal scope of the resurrection: the one who has conquered death has authority over all nations and all creation. "Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved" pairs faith with its public embodiment — baptism as the sign and seal of the believing response. The signs that accompany the mission are not conditions of genuine faith but accompaniments of authentic proclamation — the same power that operated through Jesus's ministry operates through the disciples' mission. The Gospel that began with John's baptism and Jesus's proclamation of the kingdom now extends to all the world through the commissioned church.
14.christology: "So then the Lord Jesus, after he had spoken to them, was taken up into heaven and sat down at the right hand of God" (v.19). The session at the right hand is the fulfillment of Psalm 110:1, which Jesus had applied to himself in the Temple controversies (12:36) and at the trial (14:62). The entire arc of Mark's Gospel moves from the Voice from heaven at the Jordan ("You are my beloved Son," 1:11) through the Voice at the Transfiguration (9:7), the centurion's confession at the cross (15:39), and now the ascension to the Father's right hand. Jesus is the Son whose full identity is disclosed through humiliation and exaltation together — the Gospel that begins with the inbreaking of God's kingly rule ends with the king enthroned, still working through his sent-out witnesses.
